text: The surgeon inspects the pelvic region, revealing the bladder cuff and the sigmoid colon.

The video shows the surgical field where previous excisions of endometriosis have been completed, and a right salping oophorectomy has been performed. A circled area highlights the invasive endometriosis visible on the bowel.
The surgeon palpates the area to visually identify the upper and lower margins of the endometriotic nodule on the bowel.

The surgeon places a straight suture through the upper and lower margins of the nodule, preparing it to be drawn into the stapling device. Alternatively, the suture can be placed directly through the mass for the same purpose. The suture is shortened for easier management.
The surgeon advances a circular stapler into the rectum, positioning it to draw the identified nodule into the stapling device for excision.
The surgeon opens the circular stapling device and carefully positions the nodule within it. The stapler is then closed, securely drawing the nodule into the device.

The surgeon fires the circular stapler after confirming the bowel's position is correct, ensuring the mesentery is not rotated. The nodule is excised, with pressure applied in a posterior direction to facilitate the excision of the anterior wall using the stapling device.

After removing the stapling device, the surgeon performs a sigmoidoscopy to visually inspect the anastomosis site for any signs of bleeding. The anastomosis site is carefully examined, followed by an intra-abdominal inspection to confirm the absence of bleeding. An anastomosis leak test is then conducted to ensure the integrity of the surgical connection.

The surgeon uses an atraumatic bowel grasper to clamp the bowel, fills the pelvis with fluid, and then introduces air into the bowel using a sigmoidoscope to check for air leakage at the reanastomosis site.
The surgeon removes excess fluid from the surgical field, completes any remaining procedures, and concludes the colectomy.
